macro_segments.title: The Tanna Kamma vs. Rabbi Yehuda debate on beam and opening dimensions, and the Heichal-based approach of Rav

micro_segments.title: Setting up the debate: Tanna Kamma's limits (20 high, 10 wide) vs. Rabbi Yehuda's position
Rav's approach: learning from the Heichal's dimensions (20 by 10)
Tzurat HaPetach with doorframe: does it allow wider openings?
The problem of Amaltera (cornice): if based on Heichal, why does it permit height above 20?

macro_segments.title: Resolution of the Amaltera problem: two competing approaches (Rav's Heichal-based vs. Rav Nachman's Heker-based)

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's claim that Amaltera is a disputed Baraita with uncertain source
Abaye's response: the Amaltera is taught by authoritative sources
Rav's position: conflicting Baraitot in the sources themselves
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak's alternative: it's all about Heker (recognizability), not defining a doorway like the Heichal
The Gemara's explanation of the Korah's true purpose: to be noticeable (Heker) when crossing boundaries

macro_segments.title: Comparison to Sukkah to validate the Heker approach: same principle explains both debates

micro_segments.title: Rabbah's teaching on Sukkah: one must recognize the Sechach to be aware of sitting in the Sukkah
The problem: identical debates in both Sukkah and Mavoi—why teach both if based on the same principle?
Gemara's resolution: Rabbi Yehuda's reasoning differs between the two (sitting vs. walking)
Definition of Amaltera: Kini (corner molding) vs. Piski d'Arza (horizontal molding)
Why Amaltera works as recognizable: people know to look up at the distinctive molding, unlike plain Sechach

macro_segments.title: Partial beam/Sechach above 20 Amot: when is it valid and why the different rules for Sukkah vs. Mavoi

micro_segments.title: Case of part of beam below 20 and part above: Rava rules it's Kosher for Mavoi but Pasul for Sukkah
Mavoi is Kosher: we can consider only the lower part as if it were metal (treating it with full strength despite being partial)
Sukkah is Pasul: the lower part alone wouldn't provide enough shade (Chamasa meruba m'Tzilatah)
Rava's second explanation: Sukkah is for a Yachid (individual) who won't remember if status changes; Mavoi is for Rabbim (many) who remind each other
Ravina's alternative: Sukkah is Biblical (stricter), Mavoi is Rabbinic (more lenient with safeguards)

macro_segments.title: Reconciling the debates: height is defined by the opening/space, not by the beam or Sechach themselves

micro_segments.title: Rava's conclusion: it's about the height of the space (Challal) not the height of the beam (Kora) or Sechach
Rav Pappa brings a Baraita supporting Rava: the opening (Heichal's door) was 20 Amot high
Challenge: Baraita says place the beam from the 20-line downward (Lemata), implying it must be below 20
Reinterpretation: Lemata means both upper and lower limits are about the space (Challal), not the beam's position

macro_segments.title: Technical debate on measurement: different Amah sizes (5 vs. 6 Tefachim) and when to apply them

micro_segments.title: Abaye in Rav Nachman's name: use 5-Tefach Amah for dimensions that disqualify; use 6-Tefach Amah for dimensions that qualify
Application to Mavoi: height and width measurements (disqualifying) use short Amah; length measurement (qualifying) uses long Amah
Application to Sukkah: height and bending wall measurements (disqualifying) use short Amah; length (qualifying) uses long Amah
Application to Kilayim: most measurements are about allowing planting (qualifying), so use long (6-Tefach) Amah
Case of tightly planted vineyard: use short Amah for leniency (to say it's not a Kerem), but Chachamim reject this logic
Rava's alternative: all measurements use standard 6-Tefach Amah; variation comes from loose vs. tight measurement, not from different standard sizes
